label: Training & Fine-Tuning Foundation Models — Part A

questions.stem: What type of learning is used during the pre-training phase of a foundation model?

options.text: Self-supervised learning using huge amounts of unstructured data
Supervised learning using labeled datasets
Reinforcement learning using human feedback
Semi-supervised learning using partially labeled data

explanation.correct: Pre-training trains the LLM by using huge amounts of unstructured data with self-supervised learning. During this phase, the model learns the fundamentals of language from massive datasets without explicit labels.

distractors.B: Supervised learning with labeled data describes fine-tuning, not pre-training. Pre-training uses self-supervised learning.
distractors.C: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a fine-tuning technique applied after pre-training.
distractors.D: Semi-supervised learning uses a mix of labeled and unlabeled data. Pre-training uses self-supervised learning on unstructured data.

questions.stem: What type of learning is used during fine-tuning of a foundation model?

options.text: Supervised learning using a dataset of labeled examples to update model weights
Unsupervised learning using unlabeled data
Self-supervised learning on the entire internet
Transfer learning that replaces all original weights

explanation.correct: Fine-tuning is a supervised learning process that uses a dataset of labeled examples to update the weights of the LLM, adapting it to specific tasks or domains.

distractors.B: Unsupervised learning on unlabeled data describes pre-training. Fine-tuning requires labeled examples.
distractors.C: Self-supervised learning on large data is pre-training. Fine-tuning uses specific labeled datasets for tasks.
distractors.D: Fine-tuning updates weights but doesn't replace all original weights. It extends training, not restarts it.

questions.stem: What is catastrophic forgetting in the context of fine-tuning?

options.text: When fine-tuning modifies the original LLM weights, improving single-task performance but degrading performance on other tasks
When the model loses all its weights due to a hardware failure
When the training data is accidentally deleted during fine-tuning
When the model forgets the prompt provided by the user

explanation.correct: Catastrophic forgetting happens when fine-tuning modifies the weights of the original LLM, improving performance on the single fine-tuned task but degrading performance on other tasks the model could previously handle.

distractors.B: Hardware failure is an infrastructure issue, not catastrophic forgetting. The term specifically refers to knowledge loss from weight updates.
distractors.C: Data deletion is operational, not catastrophic forgetting. The issue is about model weights being overwritten during training.
distractors.D: Forgetting prompts would be a context window issue. Catastrophic forgetting is about the model losing previously learned capabilities.

questions.stem: What is the key advantage of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)?

options.text: It freezes the original model weights and fine-tunes only a small number of task-specific adapter layers, reducing compute and memory requirements
It trains every parameter in the model simultaneously for maximum accuracy
It eliminates the need for any training data
It doubles the model size by adding a complete copy of all parameters

explanation.correct: PEFT freezes the original LLM parameters and fine-tunes only a small number of task-specific adapter layers. This significantly reduces the compute and memory needed compared to full fine-tuning.

distractors.B: Training every parameter is full fine-tuning, not PEFT. PEFT specifically avoids updating all parameters.
distractors.C: PEFT still requires training data for the adapter layers. It reduces compute, not data requirements.
distractors.D: PEFT adds only small adapter layers, not a complete parameter copy. The efficiency comes from training few parameters.

questions.stem: How does Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) work as a PEFT technique?

options.text: It preserves the original model weights and creates new trainable low-rank matrices injected into each layer of the transformer architecture
It reduces the model's layers by half to speed up training
It replaces all attention mechanisms with simple linear layers
It compresses the entire model into a single dense layer

explanation.correct: LoRA preserves (freezes) the original foundation model weights and creates new trainable low-rank matrices that are injected into each layer of the transformer architecture. Only these small matrices are trained.

distractors.B: LoRA does not remove layers. It adds small trainable matrices alongside existing layers while keeping the original intact.
distractors.C: LoRA does not replace attention mechanisms. It adds low-rank matrices to existing transformer layers.
distractors.D: LoRA does not compress the model. It adds minimal trainable parameters while keeping the full architecture.

questions.stem: Which are types or approaches to fine-tuning a foundation model? (Select THREE)

options.text: Instruction-based fine-tuning using labeled examples for specific tasks
Domain adaptation fine-tuning using limited domain-specific data
Multitask fine-tuning using examples of inputs and outputs for multiple tasks
Deletion fine-tuning that removes unwanted knowledge from the model
Compression fine-tuning that reduces model parameters by 90%
Reverse fine-tuning that undoes previous training

explanation.correct: Fine-tuning approaches include instruction-based (labeled examples for specific tasks), domain adaptation (adapting to domain-specific language/data), and multitask (training on examples for multiple tasks simultaneously to mitigate catastrophic forgetting).

distractors.D: There is no 'deletion fine-tuning.' Once trained, you cannot selectively delete knowledge from model weights.
distractors.E: Model compression is a separate technique (pruning, quantization). It is not a fine-tuning approach.
distractors.F: Reverse fine-tuning is not a recognized technique. You cannot undo training by running it in reverse.

questions.stem: How is the training dataset divided for the fine-tuning process? (Select THREE)

options.text: Training split — used to update model weights through supervised learning
Validation split — used to measure performance during fine-tuning
Test split — used for final performance evaluation after fine-tuning is complete
Production split — used to serve real-time inference in production
Archive split — stored in S3 Glacier for compliance only
Deletion split — data that is removed after training completes

explanation.correct: The dataset is divided into training (updates weights), validation (measures performance during training to provide validation accuracy), and test (final evaluation after fine-tuning to provide test accuracy).

distractors.D: Production serving is a deployment concern. Dataset splits are for training and evaluation, not inference serving.
distractors.E: Archive storage is a lifecycle concern. The three splits are for training, validation, and testing during fine-tuning.
distractors.F: There is no deletion split. All three splits serve specific roles in the training and evaluation process.

questions.stem: Which AWS services can be used for data preparation when fine-tuning a model? (Select THREE)

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Canvas for low-code data flows and feature engineering
Amazon SageMaker Clarify to detect potential biases in training data
Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth to manage data labeling workflows
Amazon CloudFront to cache training data at edge locations
Amazon Route 53 to route data to the nearest training cluster
AWS WAF to filter malicious training examples

explanation.correct: SageMaker Canvas provides low-code data preparation and feature engineering. SageMaker Clarify detects bias in training data (imbalanced representations, labeling biases). Ground Truth manages labeling workflows for training datasets.

distractors.D: CloudFront is a CDN for content delivery, not a data preparation tool for ML training.
distractors.E: Route 53 is a DNS service. It does not prepare or route data for model training.
distractors.F: WAF is a web application firewall. It protects web applications, not training data preparation.

questions.stem: Why is continuous pre-training important for foundation models? (Select TWO)

options.text: Models become more powerful over time by accumulating wider knowledge and adaptability from diverse topics, genres, and contexts
It helps models learn to use out-of-domain data better, creating value for organizations
It eliminates the need for any evaluation metrics or benchmarks
It guarantees the model will never produce harmful outputs
It replaces the need for fine-tuning entirely

explanation.correct: Continuous pre-training makes models more powerful by accumulating wider knowledge from diverse data (topics, genres, contexts). Models learn to use out-of-domain data better, creating value through adaptability.

distractors.C: Continuous pre-training makes validation harder (non-deterministic output). Metrics and benchmarks remain essential.
distractors.D: No training approach guarantees zero harmful outputs. Guardrails and monitoring are still needed.
distractors.E: Continuous pre-training and fine-tuning serve different purposes. Fine-tuning for specific tasks is still needed.

questions.stem: What are the compute requirements when fully fine-tuning a foundation model? (Select TWO)

options.text: You must load model parameters plus additional memory for optimizer, gradients, forward activations, and temporal memory
These components can add additional bytes of GPU memory for each model parameter, increasing costs
Full fine-tuning requires only CPU compute with no GPU memory needed
Full fine-tuning uses less memory than inference because fewer computations are needed
The optimizer and gradients are stored on disk only, not in GPU memory

explanation.correct: Full fine-tuning requires loading model parameters plus additional GPU memory for optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, and temporal memory. These add multiple bytes per parameter, significantly increasing compute costs.

distractors.C: Fine-tuning foundation models requires GPUs. The massive matrix operations cannot be efficiently performed on CPUs alone.
distractors.D: Fine-tuning uses more memory than inference because it needs to store gradients, optimizer states, and activations in addition to weights.
distractors.E: Optimizer and gradients must be in GPU memory for efficient computation. Disk access would be far too slow for training.

questions.scenario: A company fine-tunes a general-purpose LLM exclusively on medical terminology and clinical notes. After fine-tuning, the model excels at medical tasks but can no longer handle basic customer support conversations it previously managed well.
questions.stem: What caused this loss of general capability?

options.text: Catastrophic forgetting — fine-tuning on medical data modified the weights in ways that improved medical performance but degraded other capabilities
The model ran out of storage space for new knowledge
Medical data is incompatible with language models
The model was accidentally deleted and replaced with a medical-only model

explanation.correct: This is catastrophic forgetting — fine-tuning modified the original LLM weights to optimize for medical tasks, which degraded performance on other previously learned tasks like customer support.

distractors.B: Models don't have a fixed 'storage space' for knowledge. Catastrophic forgetting occurs because weight updates for one task can overwrite patterns needed for others.
distractors.C: Medical data is perfectly compatible with language models. The issue is that single-task fine-tuning can override general knowledge.
distractors.D: The model wasn't replaced. Its weights were modified through fine-tuning, causing it to lose general capabilities.

questions.scenario: An AI team wants to fine-tune their foundation model to handle reviews, summarization, and code translation simultaneously. They're concerned about catastrophic forgetting if they fine-tune for each task separately.
questions.stem: Which fine-tuning approach helps mitigate catastrophic forgetting when adapting to multiple tasks?

options.text: Multitask fine-tuning — training on examples of inputs and outputs for multiple tasks simultaneously
Sequential single-task fine-tuning on each task one at a time
Removing all pre-trained weights before fine-tuning
Using only zero-shot prompting without any fine-tuning

explanation.correct: Multitask fine-tuning uses training datasets with examples for multiple tasks (reviews, summarization, translation, etc.) simultaneously. This produces an instruction-tuned model that learns multiple tasks and helps mitigate catastrophic forgetting.

distractors.B: Sequential single-task fine-tuning can cause catastrophic forgetting — each subsequent task may overwrite patterns from the previous one.
distractors.C: Removing pre-trained weights loses all learned knowledge. You want to preserve general capability while adding task-specific skills.
distractors.D: Zero-shot avoids fine-tuning entirely. The question asks about a fine-tuning approach that handles multiple tasks.

questions.scenario: A startup with limited GPU budget needs to fine-tune a 70-billion parameter model for their customer service application. Full fine-tuning would exceed their compute budget by 5x.
questions.stem: Which technique allows them to adapt the model within their budget constraints?

options.text: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) — freezes original weights and trains only small adapter layers, reducing compute and memory requirements
Pre-training the model from scratch on a smaller dataset
Increasing the GPU budget by requesting more funding
Deploying the model without any customization

explanation.correct: PEFT reduces compute and memory requirements by freezing original model parameters and training only a small number of task-specific adapter layers. This makes fine-tuning large models feasible with limited GPU budgets.

distractors.B: Pre-training from scratch is far more expensive than full fine-tuning. It would far exceed the budget.
distractors.C: Requesting more budget is a business decision, not a technical technique. PEFT solves the technical constraint.
distractors.D: No customization means the model won't be adapted for customer service. PEFT provides customization within budget.

questions.scenario: A pharmaceutical company needs their foundation model to understand drug names, molecular structures, and clinical trial terminology that wasn't well represented in the model's original training data.
questions.stem: Which fine-tuning approach adapts the model to this specialized vocabulary?

options.text: Domain adaptation fine-tuning — adapting the pre-trained model to specific tasks using limited domain-specific data including industry jargon and technical terms
Reducing the model's temperature to improve medical accuracy
Adding guardrails to block non-medical responses
Increasing the model's context window to fit more medical text

explanation.correct: Domain adaptation fine-tuning adapts pre-trained foundation models to specific tasks using limited domain-specific data. It helps the model work with industry jargon, technical terms, and specialized data like pharmaceutical terminology.

distractors.B: Temperature controls randomness, not domain knowledge. The model needs to learn medical vocabulary through fine-tuning.
distractors.C: Guardrails filter content but don't teach the model new domain knowledge. The model needs training on medical terms.
distractors.D: Context window size is an architecture constraint. The model needs to learn the meaning of medical terms, not just fit more text.

questions.scenario: A company using Amazon Bedrock wants to customize Amazon Titan Text models with their proprietary unlabeled data without the cost and complexity of full fine-tuning with labeled examples.
questions.stem: Which Amazon Bedrock capability allows customization using unlabeled data?

options.text: Continued pre-training in Amazon Bedrock — customizes Titan Text models using unlabeled data in a secure managed environment
RAG with knowledge bases — retrieves data at inference time
Guardrails — filters inappropriate content from responses
Amazon Bedrock Agents — orchestrates multi-step tasks

explanation.correct: Continued pre-training in Amazon Bedrock lets you customize Amazon Titan Text Express and Titan Text Lite using your own unlabeled data in a secure, managed environment without needing labeled examples.

distractors.B: RAG retrieves information at inference time but doesn't modify the model's weights or knowledge.
distractors.C: Guardrails filter content but don't add domain knowledge to the model.
distractors.D: Agents orchestrate actions with external systems. They don't customize the model's internal knowledge.

questions.scenario: A machine learning engineer is performing fine-tuning on a foundation model and wants to understand the training loop.
questions.stem: What happens during each iteration of the fine-tuning training loop? (Select TWO)

options.text: Prompts from the training dataset are passed to the LLM to generate completions
The distribution of completions is compared to training labels to calculate a loss between token distributions
The model architecture is automatically restructured based on loss values
New layers are permanently added to the model after each batch
The training data is automatically augmented with synthetic examples

explanation.correct: During fine-tuning, prompts are selected from training data and passed to the LLM to generate completions. Then the distribution of completions is compared to training labels to calculate a loss, which is used to update weights.

distractors.C: Model architecture remains fixed during fine-tuning. Only weights are updated, not the structure.
distractors.D: Layers are not added during fine-tuning (except in PEFT adapter layers which are added once, not per batch).
distractors.E: Automatic data augmentation is not part of the standard fine-tuning loop described. Data is prepared before training begins.

questions.scenario: A data engineering team needs to prepare data for fine-tuning. They need scalable processing, feature storage, and data labeling capabilities.
questions.stem: Which AWS services match these three specific data preparation needs? (Select THREE)

options.text: Amazon EMR or AWS Glue interactive sessions for scalable data processing with Apache Spark
Amazon SageMaker Feature Store for feature discovery and centralized storage
Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth for managing data labeling workflows
Amazon CloudWatch for processing large datasets
AWS IAM for labeling training examples
Amazon S3 Glacier for real-time feature serving

explanation.correct: Amazon EMR/Glue provides scalable Apache Spark-based processing. Feature Store provides centralized feature discovery and storage. Ground Truth manages data labeling workflows for training datasets.

distractors.D: CloudWatch monitors metrics and logs. It does not process training data at scale.
distractors.E: IAM manages access permissions. It does not provide data labeling capabilities.
distractors.F: S3 Glacier is archival storage with slow retrieval. It is not suitable for real-time feature serving.

questions.scenario: An ML team is comparing LoRA to full fine-tuning for adapting a large language model. They want to understand the technical differences.
questions.stem: Which statements about LoRA are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: LoRA preserves the original model weights and only trains new low-rank matrices
PEFT and LoRA modify the weights of the model but not the representations
LoRA replaces the original model weights with new random weights
LoRA requires more GPU memory than full fine-tuning
LoRA can only be applied to the final layer of the model

explanation.correct: LoRA preserves (freezes) original weights and trains only new low-rank matrices injected into transformer layers. PEFT techniques including LoRA modify the model's weights (through adapters) but not its representations (semantic encodings).

distractors.C: LoRA freezes original weights. It does not replace them. The original model remains intact.
distractors.D: LoRA requires significantly less GPU memory than full fine-tuning because only small matrices are trained.
distractors.E: LoRA injects trainable matrices into each layer of the transformer, not just the final layer.

questions.scenario: A company wants to fine-tune a text generation model on their domain-specific dataset. They want a managed service that provides pre-trained models and fine-tuning infrastructure.
questions.stem: Which AWS service provides the capability to fine-tune large language models on domain-specific datasets?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
Amazon Bedrock Guardrails
AWS CloudFormation
Amazon CloudWatch

explanation.correct: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart provides the capability to fine-tune large language models, particularly text generation models, on domain-specific datasets to improve performance in specific domains.

distractors.B: Guardrails filter content at inference time. They don't provide fine-tuning infrastructure.
distractors.C: CloudFormation deploys infrastructure as code. It doesn't fine-tune language models.
distractors.D: CloudWatch monitors metrics and logs. It doesn't provide model training or fine-tuning.

questions.scenario: A research team explores a fine-tuning approach that preserves the base model weights entirely and only intervenes on the hidden representations at specific layers. They want to leverage the linear representation hypothesis.
questions.stem: Which fine-tuning technique operates on hidden representations rather than model weights?

options.text: Representation Fine-Tuning (ReFT) — freezes the base model and learns task-specific interventions on hidden representations
LoRA — injects trainable low-rank matrices into transformer layers
Full fine-tuning — updates every model parameter
Prompt tuning — optimizes a continuous embedding in the prompt space

explanation.correct: ReFT freezes the base model and learns task-specific interventions on hidden representations. It leverages the linear representation hypothesis — that concepts are encoded in linear subspaces of representations in neural networks.

distractors.B: LoRA modifies weights through low-rank matrices, not representations. PEFT/LoRA modify weights but not representations.
distractors.C: Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters (weights), not specifically targeting hidden representations.
distractors.D: Prompt tuning optimizes an input embedding, not hidden representations within the model layers.
